Sleep % vs treatment — methods & rationale

A full account of how the five-arm Olden Labs wakefulness study was analyzed: the reasoning behind the design choices, an explanation of every test used, the assumption checks, the result, and a power analysis for what it would take to confirm the lead signal.

Linear mixed modelCage random effect Dunnett vs placeboKenward–Roger dfPower analysis
Contents: 1 Study & question·2 Why this is hard·3 Pipeline· 4 Tests used·5 Assumptions·6 Result· 7 Steel comparison·8 Power·9 Caveats·10 Reproducibility·11 References
TLDR: We found that a significant portion of variance is explained by inter-cage differences, and thus mice in a cage cannot be assumed independent. The current method runs a Steel's test assuming all mice are independent, which produces overconfident p-values. Grouping by cages, while accounting for cage-level effects, does not fully utilize information available and results in too-conservative estimates. Instead, we can use a linear mixed-effects model with cage as a random effect, comparing each treatment to placebo with Dunnett's correction and Kenward–Roger small-sample degrees of freedom. Using AMEN as an example study, we can also perform a power analysis which shows ~15 cages/group would be needed to show significance.

1 · The study & the scientific question

This is a wake-promotion screen. AMENv1 (a peptide, tested both buffered and non-buffered) is the candidate; caffeine is a positive control (a known wake-promoter); TFA + ballast and a subcutaneous placebo are negative/vehicle controls. The biological question is simple: does AMENv1 reduce sleep relative to placebo, and ideally does it approach caffeine? The right outcome is therefore sleep % of time (a wake-promoter should lower it), measured in the hours after dosing.

2 · The unit of analysis

An observation, for a statistical test, must be an independent piece of information. The machinery — standard errors, p-values, confidence intervals — assumes each data point varies on its own. Mice in the same cage are not independent. They share one water bottle, one bedding, one rack position, the same cagemates, the same draft or light gradient. If something nudges Cage 7, all five mice move together. They carry less than five independent pieces of information — clustering, measured by the intra-class correlation (ICC).

Treating each mouse as independent is pseudoreplication. Feeding 95 "independent" mice into a test when you really have ~19 units produces standard errors too small, confidence intervals too narrow, and p-values too optimistic. It manufactures significance the experiment never earned, and it is exactly what inflated the stars on the previous versions of these plots.

Evidence: how strong is the clustering?

Variance is simply a number for "how spread out are the values?" Mice sleep very different amounts, and variance puts a size on that scatter. "Explaining the variance" means asking where the scatter comes from. Some of it is because whole cages differ from one another (call it between-cage scatter), and some is because individual mice differ from their own cagemates (within-cage scatter). Sorting the total scatter into those two buckets is the whole game here.

The intra-class correlation (ICC) is that split written as one fraction — between-cage variance divided by total variance:

ICC = σ²cage / ( σ²cage + σ²within ) = 57.4 / ( 57.4 + 28.9 ) = 0.67

(σ²cage = 57.4 and σ²within = 28.9 are the two variances the model estimates.) ICC runs from 0 to 1 and answers "how much of the spread is due to the cage rather than the individual mouse?" — equivalently, "how alike are two mice from the same cage?" ICC = 0 would mean cagemates are no more similar than random mice (cage irrelevant); ICC = 1 would mean every mouse in a cage is identical. Here ICC = 0.67: about two-thirds of all the variation in sleep is between cages — cagemates are strikingly alike. The plot makes it clear: mice huddle around their cage mean (thick bar), while the cage means themselves scatter widely.

Is that cage effect real, or could it be coincidence? We test it formally with a likelihood-ratio test (LRT), which pits two hypotheses against each other. Let σ²cage stand for the between-cage variance — a number for how much cages truly differ. The two hypotheses are:

H0 (null):  σ²cage = 0  —  cages do not differ; the clustering is illusory
H1 (alternative):  σ²cage > 0  —  cages genuinely differ

We fit the data under each hypothesis and record each model's log-likelihood ℓ — a score of how well the model matches the data (higher = better fit). H1 is the full model sleep_pct ~ 1 + (1|cage); H0 is the same model with the cage term removed. The test statistic is twice the gap between the two scores:

LR = −2 ln( L0 / L1 ) = 2 ( ℓ1 − ℓ0 )
= 2 × ( −2044.8 − (−2059.5) ) = 2 × 14.7 = 29.4

If H0 were true, this statistic would follow a chi-squared distribution with 1 degree of freedom (the "1" = the single extra parameter, σ²cage, that H1 adds). The p-value is the probability of seeing a value at least this large when H0 holds:

p = P( χ²1 ≥ 29.4 ) = 5.9 × 10−8  (< 1 in 10 million)

So we reject H0 decisively: a cage effect this strong would essentially never appear by chance. The clustering is unmistakably real, and cage must be in the model.

How to read this chart Left panel: each vertical column is one cage, grouped and coloured by treatment arm. Every faint dot is a single mouse's sleep% over the first 6 hours. The thick horizontal bar is that cage's average. The thing to notice: dots within a column sit close together, but the bars (cage averages) jump up and down a lot — mice resemble their own cagemates far more than mice in other cages. Right panel: the same fact boiled down to one number. The tall dark portion (67%) is how much of the total spread in sleep comes from differences between cages rather than between individual mice.

There are three ways to respect this, and choosing among them is the central design decision:

① Per-mouse tests
✗ wrong
Treat all 95 mice as independent. Maximum apparent power, but invalid — pseudoreplication. Produced the original false stars.
② Cage means
✓ safe
Collapse each cage to one number, then test the ~19 cage values. Bulletproof and trivially defensible, but throws away within-cage information and the time course, leaving 3–4 points per group.
③ Mixed model
✓ best
Keep every mouse, but add cage as a random effect so the model discounts the clustering and recovers the correct effective sample size. Our primary, with ② as a sensitivity check.
Why not just collapse to cage means and be done? At 3–4 cages/group the cage-mean route is honest but low-powered, and it cannot test whether the effect builds over the night — the most informative feature here. The mixed model keeps that while staying honest, so it is primary; the cage-mean Dunnett confirms the two agree.

Evidence: what ignoring it does to the p-values

Treating all ~20 mice per group as independent (the original approach) produces dramatic false precision. For the lead arm, the per-mouse test returns p ≈ 0.003 — a confident three-star "hit" — while the honest cage-level test on the very same data gives p ≈ 0.28–0.48. The stars are an artifact of counting 95 mice when there are really ~19 independent units.

Here is exactly why. The precision of a group average is its standard error, SE = s / √n — so the sample size n you claim directly controls how confident you look (bigger n → smaller SE → bigger test statistic → smaller p). Clustering shrinks the effective sample size by the design effect DEFF = 1 + (m − 1)·ICC, where m = mice per cage:

neff = n / [ 1 + (m − 1)·ICC ] = 20 / [ 1 + (5 − 1)×0.67 ] = 20 / 3.68 ≈ 5.4

So 20 mice carry only about as much information as ~5 independent animals. Counting them as a full 20 shrinks the SE by a factor √(20 / 5.4) ≈ 1.9 — that spurious ~1.9× boost in the test statistic is precisely what turns a true p ≈ 0.3 into a fake p ≈ 0.003. The effect appears for both the rank (Steel) and parametric (Dunnett) versions:

How to read this chart Two panels, one for each kind of test (we'll explain both in §4). In each, the four treatments sit along the bottom. For every treatment there are two bars: the red bar pretends each mouse is an independent data point (the wrong, "pseudoreplicated" way — ~20 per group); the blue bar correctly counts each cage once (~4 per group). Bar height is the p-value. The dashed orange line is the 0.05 "significant" threshold; ✱ marks bars that cross it. Look at AMEN (non-buf.): the red bar plunges to p≈0.003 (significant!), but the honest blue bar on the identical data stays up near 0.3–0.5. The red "significance" is pure illusion created by over-counting mice.

3 · The analysis pipeline

4 · The tests used in this analysis

The full list of components is below — what each does, how it works, and its role. Badges: PRIMARY, COMPONENT (a piece of the primary), SENSITIVITY (confirmatory cross-check). Click any test to expand it.

Linear mixed-effects model (LMM)Primary
Purpose Estimate the treatment effects while correctly accounting for the fact that mice are clustered in cages — so the standard errors and p-values come out honest.

The core of the analysis: sleep_pct ~ treatment*hour + (1|cage).

A mixed model splits each mouse's sleep into fixed effects (treatment, time, and their interaction — what we care about) and random effects (cage — the grouping we must respect). The cage random intercept gives every cage its own offset drawn from a single bell-curve; estimating that one cage-to-cage variance — rather than 19 separate cage constants — is what lets the model keep all 95 mice while crediting only the ~19 independent units.

Written out, for mouse m in cage c at hour h:

sleepcmh = β0 + τtreat + βh(h − h̄) + γtreat(h − h̄) + uc + εcmh
uc ~ N(0, σ²cage),   εcmh ~ N(0, σ²resid)

The β/τ/γ are fixed effects (τ = treatment offsets, βh = shared hour slope, γ = per-treatment slope adjustments — the interaction); uc is the random cage offset and ε the leftover. The two key assumptions are the two normal distributions on the right. REML estimates σ²cage = 42.9 and σ²resid = 274.6.

Shrinkage / partial pooling
Extreme cages are pulled gently toward the overall mean in proportion to how few mice they hold and how noisy they are — stabilizing estimates.
ICC (and why it differs from §2)
In this hourly model ICCcage ≈ 0.14. That looks much smaller than the 0.67 in §2, but it is not a contradiction: here each "leftover" also contains a mouse's hour-to-hour wobble, which swells the total spread and so shrinks the cage fraction. Measured on one summary number per mouse (§2), the same clustering reads as 0.67. Either way cage matters — the two numbers differ only in what gets counted in the denominator. The mouse-within-cage variance estimated to zero, so that term was dropped.
treatment × hour
Tests directly whether the gap grows over the night, and avoids a second multiplicity layer from six separate per-hour tests.
Why primary
The only approach that fixes pseudoreplication, keeps all the data, and characterizes the time course at once.
REML estimationComponent
Purpose Estimate the two variance components (cage and residual) with a small-sample bias correction — these are what every downstream standard error is built from.

How the mixed model's numbers are found.

Restricted maximum likelihood iteratively searches for the variance components (cage variance, residual variance) that make the observed data most likely, then solves for the fixed effects. "Restricted" corrects the variance estimates for the fact that the fixed effects were also estimated — important when clusters are few. (Our hand-rolled REML reproduced R's lme4 variance components exactly, a cross-check that the fit is correct.)

One-way ANOVA & the omnibus F-testComponent
Purpose Give one overall yes/no test of whether a whole term (treatment, hour, or their interaction) matters at all, before looking at any individual arm.

"Is there any difference among the five arms?"

ANOVA partitions variance into between- vs within-group and forms an F-ratio; a large F means the means differ by more than noise. It is an omnibus test — it says something differs, not which. Here it appears as the Kenward–Roger F-tests on the model's fixed effects (treatment, hour, treatment×hour), providing overall significance and gating interpretation; the specific arm comparisons come from Dunnett.

Dunnett's many-to-one testPrimary
Purpose Compare each treatment to placebo while holding the chance of any false positive across the four comparisons at 5%.

The treatment-vs-placebo contrasts.

In plain terms. Picture checking four treatments against placebo with four separate fairness tests — each carries a 1-in-20 chance of a false alarm, so across four you are fairly likely to get at least one just by luck. Dunnett bundles all four into a single procedure that holds the overall false-alarm rate at 1-in-20, while still telling you which individual arms differ from placebo. It is the standard choice whenever you have one control and several treatments.

After the model is fit, Dunnett extracts exactly the comparisons of interest — each active arm against the single placebo control — and corrects their joint false-positive rate. Because all four share the same control, the contrasts are correlated; Dunnett accounts for that rather than treating them as four independent tests.

Mechanically, for treatment k vs control 0 the test statistic is

tk = ( μ̂k − μ̂0 ) / SE( μ̂k − μ̂0 )

and arm k is declared different from placebo if |tk| exceeds the Dunnett critical value dα,K,ρ,df, read from the multivariate-t distribution for K = 4 correlated comparisons (their correlation is ρ = ½ when group sizes are equal, because all four share one control). The hour-6 gap itself is a linear combination of the fitted coefficients:

gapk(h=6) = τk + γk(6 − h̄)
AMENnon-buf: −9.13 + (−1.81)(6 − 3.59) = −9.13 − 4.37 = −13.5
Why Dunnett (not Tukey)
We have a designated control and only care about treatment-vs-placebo. Spending the error budget on those four comparisons is more powerful than all-pairs.
Kenward–Roger degrees of freedomComponent
Purpose Shrink the degrees of freedom (and nudge the SEs up) to reflect the small number of cages, so the p-values are not over-optimistic.

The small-sample correction that makes the p-values honest.

With only ~19 cages, the naïve large-sample reference distribution is anti-conservative — it assumes more independent information than exists. Kenward–Roger adjusts the standard errors and effective degrees of freedom downward to reflect the small number of clusters, the standard mixed-model fix.

Effect here
Widens confidence intervals and raises p-values relative to the naïve fit — the correct, conservative behavior at this cluster count.
Multivariate-t (mvt) correctionComponent
Purpose Compute Dunnett’s joint cutoff exactly from the four comparisons’ correlation structure, instead of an approximation.

How Dunnett's joint critical value is computed.

The exact Dunnett adjustment integrates the multivariate-t distribution defined by the contrasts' correlation structure, giving precise simultaneous p-values and confidence intervals rather than an approximation (such as "dunnettx").

Cage-mean Dunnett (summary-measure)Sensitivity
Purpose A simpler, assumption-light cross-check — collapse each cage to one number and re-test — to confirm the mixed model did not manufacture its result.

The bulletproof confirmatory analysis.

Collapse each cage to a single 0–6 h sleep % and run one-way + Dunnett on those ~19 values. It makes zero distributional assumptions about mouse-level data and is immune to any clustering critique — but it discards the time course and is lower-powered.

Role
Run alongside the mixed model; the two agree in direction and significance, which is what makes the conclusion robust. The figure-ready number for a skeptical reviewer.
Cage-level permutation testSensitivity
Purpose A distribution-free check on the p-values that needs no normality and no degrees-of-freedom assumption.

Distribution-free inference for the mixed model.

Shuffle the treatment labels across the 19 cages many times, refit, and see how often an effect as large as observed appears by chance. It assumes nothing about normality or degrees of freedom — the exact, offline substitute for Kenward–Roger — and confirmed the KR results (the lead arm's per-contrast p rose from the naïve 0.025 to a corrected ~0.07–0.11).

Why not the other tests?

Tests considered and deliberately not chosen — the wrong approach (pseudoreplication), the all-pairs and nonparametric alternatives, and the contingency models for if assumptions had failed. Click any to expand.

Per-mouse t-test / Šídák-corrected pairwiseNot used
Purpose What we are deliberately avoiding: comparing arms while treating each mouse as independent (pseudoreplication), which fakes significance.

What the original graphs did.

An unpaired t-test compares two group means relative to pooled variability. Run on all 20 mice per group, it treats cagemates as independent.

Why rejected
Pseudoreplication: it counts ~5× more independent observations than exist, so its standard errors are too small and its stars inflated. The whole analysis exists to replace this.
Kruskal–Wallis testNot used
Purpose A rank-based overall test we did not use — it ignores cage clustering and is badly underpowered at this sample size.

The nonparametric twin of one-way ANOVA.

Replaces values with ranks and asks whether any group tends to rank higher — an omnibus test robust to non-normality and outliers.

Why not
It does not model the cage clustering and, like all rank methods here, is badly underpowered at 3–4 cages/group. The residual normality was adequate, so the parametric route was preferred.
Tukey's HSD (all-pairs)Not used
Purpose An all-pairs comparison we did not use, because we only care about treatment-vs-placebo, not every possible pairing.

The all-pairwise parametric post-hoc.

Tukey compares every pair of groups, correcting for all of them via the studentized-range distribution.

Why not
It would test irrelevant pairs (e.g. caffeine vs TFA), wasting the multiplicity budget and lowering power for the comparisons we want. Dunnett is the targeted choice.
Steel test & Steel–DwassAlternative
Purpose The rank-based version of Dunnett — set aside because, with only 3–4 cages per arm, it structurally cannot detect anything.

The nonparametric many-to-one / all-pairs rank tests.

In plain terms. Steel does the same job as Dunnett — compare each treatment to placebo — but first it replaces every sleep value with its rank position (smallest = 1, next = 2, and so on) and works only with those ranks. Using order instead of the actual numbers makes it immune to a single freak value, which is its appeal. The cost: with only 3–4 cages per group there are very few possible orderings, so it physically cannot produce a small p-value — it almost never detects anything at this sample size.

The Steel test is the rank-based analog of Dunnett (treatments vs one control); Steel–Dwass is the rank analog of Tukey (all pairs). Both rank the pooled data, sum ranks, and apply a joint multiplicity correction. The right tool when cage-level values are clearly non-normal.

For treatment k vs control 0: pool the two groups, rank all values, and let Wk be the sum of group k's ranks. Standardised (N = nk + n0):

zk = ( Wk − E[Wk] ) / √Var(Wk)
E[Wk] = nk(N+1)/2,   Var(Wk) = nk n0(N+1)/12

Multiplicity is handled by referring maxk|zk| to its joint null. The fatal problem at this sample size is the discreteness floor: with nk = n0 = 4 cages, the smallest two-sided p the test can ever return — even with perfect separation — is

pmin = 2 / C(nk+n0, nk) = 2 / C(8,4) = 2/70 = 0.029
Why not chosen
Two compounding penalties: ranks discard magnitude, and with 3–4 cages/arm there is a hard discreteness floor (smallest possible two-sided p ≈0.03, ≈0.1 after correction). §7 shows Steel is uniformly the most conservative option here.
GLMM (beta / binomial) & transformsContingency
Purpose The fallback if the bounded 0–100 outcome had broken the normality assumption — checked, not needed here.

If the bounded outcome had misbehaved.

Sleep % is bounded 0–100 and piles up at the edges in some hours. If residuals were badly non-normal, the fixes would be a logit / arcsine-√ transform or a generalized linear mixed model with a beta or binomial family that models the 0–1 bound honestly.

Status
Not needed — the residual QQ plot (§5) is acceptable and the permutation test is distribution-free anyway. Listed as the principled escalation for a future, edge-heavy dataset.

Reading the numbers: coefficients (gaps) vs group means (bars)

Note: the model's coefficients are not the same thing as the group means you see as bar heights. The model treats placebo as the reference, so it stores one mean plus a set of differences:

  • the intercept β0 = the placebo mean — this one coefficient really is a mean;
  • each treatment coefficient τ = that arm minus placebo — a gap, not a mean.

To recover an arm's actual mean (its bar height), you add the intercept back:

meank = β0 + τk     e.g. AMEN_nonbuf: 27.7 + (−9.1) = 18.6

So −9.1 (a coefficient = the gap) and 18.6 (a bar height = the arm's actual sleep level) describe the same arm — one is "how far below placebo," the other is "where it actually sits." The same coefficients get used two different ways:

quantitywhat it iswhere you see it
β0 + τk  (group mean)the arm's sleep levelthe bar heights (§6 plot)
τk (+ γk·hour)  (gap)difference vs placebothe contrast table + Dunnett p (§6)

emmeans produces both from the one fit: it adds the coefficients up to draw the bars (means), and keeps them as differences to compute the gaps that Dunnett tests. Bars = means; coefficients = mostly gaps; only the intercept is itself a mean.

What treatment × hour is actually for

The thing we care about is the gap between a treatment and placebo — how much less those mice sleep. The crucial fact about this experiment is that the gap is not a single number — it grows as the night goes on. For the lead arm:

AMEN (non-buf.) vs placebohour 1hour 3–4hour 6
gap in sleep %  (negative = less sleep)≈ −4≈ −9≈ −13

Early in the night AMEN barely differs from placebo; by the end it is far below. So the question "what is the AMEN effect?" has no single answer — it depends on the hour. The entire job of treatment * hour is to let the model say that: to let the gap change from hour to hour instead of being locked to one fixed value.

When you build the model you get two choices:

Without the interaction
treatment + hour
The model is forced to use one gap for every hour — e.g. −9 at hour 1, −9 at hour 3, −9 at hour 6. That is wrong at both ends: it overstates the effect early and understates it late. It physically cannot represent a gap that grows.
With the interaction
treatment * hour
The model is allowed to say "the gap starts near −4 and widens by about 1.8 points every hour." That one extra number per arm — the interaction coefficient γ — is the build-up.

So the interaction is simply permission for the gap to change over time. Three reasons it's worth having here:

  • It matches what's happening — the drug takes time to act, so the effect accumulates; a model without it would smear that into one muddy average.
  • It tells you when to look — the gap is biggest at hour 6, so that's where we report the effect (−13.5). Averaging over the whole night would dilute a real −13 down to about −9.
  • It answers a biological question — how fast does AMEN act, and is it still working by hour 6?

Geometrically, "the gap can change" shows up as each arm's line having its own tilt, so the lines fan apart rather than staying parallel. That tilt is the arm's slope, read off the fitted coefficients (h̄ = 3.59 is the average hour):

sleep(arm, h) = β0 + τarm + ( βh + γarm )( h − h̄ )

The bracket is the slope: βh is the trend placebo follows, and each treatment adds its own γ (the interaction). Plugging in the numbers, AMEN and caffeine tilt downward while placebo stays flat:

slopecaffeine = βh + γcaffeine = −0.01 + (−2.53) = −2.54 / h
slopeplacebo = βh = −0.01 / h  (essentially flat)
How to read this chart The horizontal axis is hours into the night; the vertical axis is % of time asleep. Each thick line is one treatment arm (the faint dots are the hour-by-hour averages behind it). A line sloping downward means those mice sleep less as the night goes on. The number in the legend is that line's slope — how many percentage-points of sleep are lost per hour (e.g. caffeine −2.54/h drops fastest; placebo −0.01/h is flat). Because the lines are not parallel — caffeine and AMEN dive while placebo and TFA stay level — each arm needs its own slope. That "lines fanning apart" is the interaction, made visible.

5 · Checking the assumption: residual normality

Our method makes one assumption worth checking. After the model explains what it can (treatment, time, cage), there is always some leftover wiggle in each data point — the part the model couldn't predict, called the residual. The method assumes those leftovers follow the familiar bell curve (most small, a few large, symmetric). Note: it's the leftovers that must be bell-shaped, never the raw sleep numbers.

What is a QQ plot? It is the standard eye-test for "are these leftovers bell-shaped?" It sorts the leftovers from smallest to largest and plots each one against the value a perfect bell curve would have put in that position. If reality matches the bell curve, the dots line up on a straight diagonal. Systematic curving away from the line would be the warning sign.

Read: the middle ~90% of residuals sit on the line — normal enough for the model; only the extreme tails bend slightly (the 0–100 bounding). Shapiro returns p<0.001, but at n=485 it is hypersensitive and flags trivial deviations. This does not threaten the result: Dunnett+Kenward–Roger is robust to mild non-normality, and the distribution-free permutation test agreed. No transform was needed.

6 · The result

On the time window: 0–6 h is shown here only as the night-long endpoint. The bar plot reports every cumulative window (0–1 h … 0–6 h), and the identical pipeline runs on any window — a single post-dose slice, a different cumulative range, or per-hour — by changing one variable. The effect is near-absent at 1–3 h and emerges at 4–6 h, so the window you report should match the question you are asking.
How to read this chart The bottom axis splits the night into growing windows — the first hour, the first two hours, … up to all six. Inside each window there is one bar per arm (grey = placebo). Bar height is average sleep%; the thin whisker on top shows the uncertainty. The reading rule: compare each coloured bar to grey — bars below grey mean less sleep (more wakefulness). The small number above each bar is its p-value vs placebo (lower = stronger evidence; a ✱ would mark p<0.05). Watch the orange and yellow AMEN bars: level with the others in the first 1–3 h, then dropping clearly below placebo by 4–6 h — the effect building over the night. No bar earns a star, but AMEN (non-buf.) gets closest by the late windows.
ArmGap vs placebo @ 0–6 h95% CIp (Dunnett + KR)
Caffeine−9.7[−25.3, 6.0]0.334
TFA+3.6[−12.1, 19.3]0.938
AMEN (buffered)−8.2[−25.2, 8.7]0.538
AMEN (non-buffered)−13.5[−29.2, 2.2]0.107

Kenward–Roger F-tests on the joint model: treatment F(4,14.0)=1.81, p=0.18; hour F(1,385)=5.54, p=0.019; treatment×hour F(4,385)=1.69, p=0.15. ICCcage=0.14. (The hour effect is the average slope across arms, ≈ −1%/h — significant because caffeine and AMEN decline steeply; placebo and TFA are themselves flat.) AMEN non-buffered is the lead signal — largest, most consistent gap, the only CI that nearly excludes zero — but not significant at 19 cages.

7 · Why not a nonparametric (Steel) test?

Run on the identical 0–6 h cage data, the Steel many-to-one test is uniformly the most conservative option — the largest p-value for every arm.

How to read this chart Each group along the bottom is one treatment. Within it, three bars = three different ways of running the same treatment-vs-placebo comparison. Bar height is the p-value (lower = stronger evidence / more powerful test). The dashed line is the 0.05 "significant" threshold. The point: the purple Steel bars are the tallest in every group — that method is consistently the least able to find a difference, because it works on ranks and is crippled by the tiny number of cages. The green mixed-model bars are lowest (most powerful).
ArmSteel (cage rank)Parametric Dunnett (cage)Mixed model + time (Dunnett+KR)
Caffeine0.9770.8960.334
TFA0.9770.9230.938
AMEN (buffered)0.7600.5860.538
AMEN (non-buffered)0.4660.2830.107

Conservatism ordering: mixed+time < parametric cage Dunnett < Steel (left = most power). Steel loses power because it discards magnitude and hits a discreteness floor at 3–4 cages/arm. (Full reasoning in the "Why not the other tests?" dropdown in §4.)

8 · Power analysis — how many cages would confirm it?

"Power" is the probability a study correctly flags a real effect — formally, with β the false-negative rate:

power = P( reject H0 | H1 is true ) = 1 − β

The effect is large but the study is small. We estimate power by simulation: repeatedly generate fake studies from the observed 0–6 h effect sizes and the pooled cage-to-cage SD (σ = 7.3), run the exact Dunnett test on each, and record the fraction that reach p<0.05. Repeating this across cage counts gives the curve — "at how many cages per group would each AMEN arm reach significance?"

Target powerAMEN (non-buffered), gap −9.1AMEN (buffered), gap −6.8
50%~9 cages/group~15 cages/group
80% (conventional)~15 cages/group~27 cages/group
90%~19 cages/group>30 cages/group
Key implication: at the current ~4 cages/group, power for the lead arm is only ~21% — a real effect would be missed ~4 times out of 5. Because clustering is strong (high ICC), power comes from adding cages, not mice: a sixth mouse in an existing cage barely helps; a new cage helps a lot. Confirming AMEN non-buffered needs roughly 15 cages/group (or fewer with a tighter, less variable protocol).

9 · Caveats

  • Underpowered, not null. Absence of stars ≠ absence of effect; the direction and ~10-pt size are consistent across every method.
  • Power lives in cages. With this ICC, adding mice per cage barely moves power — add cages.
  • One night. A single post-dose window; circadian/repeat-night structure is not captured here.
  • Bounded outcome. Sleep % has mass near 0/100 in some hours; the transform wasn't needed but a beta/binomial GLMM is the principled escalation if extremes dominate a future dataset.
  • Variance is noisy at 19 cages. The cage-variance estimate (and thus KR df, and the power numbers) is itself uncertain; the cage-mean Dunnett agreed, which is reassuring.
  • Confirm the design facts. Dosing time and the intended primary endpoint should be checked against the study record; this analysis assumes dosing at recording start and sleep % as the endpoint.

10 · Reproducibility

Per-mouse-per-hour sleep % built in Python; models fit in R:

m <- lmer(sleep_pct ~ treatment*hour_c + (1|cage/mouse), data=long)
emmeans(m, ~treatment, at=list(hour_c = 6-mean_hour), lmer.df="kenward-roger") |>
  contrast(method="trt.vs.ctrl", ref=1, adjust="mvt")

Software: R 4.5 · lme4 · lmerTest · emmeans · pbkrtest (via micromamba); Python 3.9 · numpy · scipy · matplotlib. Scripts: export_csv.py, gold.R, gold_windows.R, plot_final.py, compare_methods.py, power_curve.py, build_methods.py. Hand-rolled REML in pipeline.py reproduced lme4's variance components exactly.

Methodological references:

  1. Cum M, Santiago Pérez JA, Iwata RL, Lopez N, Higgs A, Li A, Ye C, Wangia E, Wright ES, García Restrepo C, Padilla-Coreano N. "A Multiparadigm Approach to Characterize Dominance Behaviors in CD1 and C57BL6 Male Mice." eNeuro 11(11), ENEURO.0342-24.2024 (2024). doi:10.1523/ENEURO.0342-24.2024  —
